title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: How to Make Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil — Easy Italian Recipe
seo.metaDescription: Learn how to make authentic Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil. This easy Italian recipe includes step-by-step instructions, tips, and serving ideas.

description: Focaccia Genovese is a true masterpiece of Italian baking, a rustic flatbread known for its incredible airy texture and golden, olive oil-rich crust. Infused with aromatic rosemary and coarse sea salt, it is a simple yet profoundly satisfying treat that captures the essence of Ligurian cuisine. Perfect as an appetizer, side dish, or light meal.

ingredients.notes: plus extra for drizzling and pan

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large bowl, combine the warm water, yeast, and granulated sugar. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy, indicating the yeast is active. This is crucial for a light and airy focaccia.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dough
instructions.text: Add the flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Stir with a wooden spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms. Gradually add 2 tablespoons of the extra virgin olive oil, incorporating it until the dough comes together. The dough will be very wet and sticky.

instructions.title: First Rise
instructions.text: Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp cloth and let the dough rise in a warm place for at least 1-2 hours, or until it has doubled in size. This slow rise develops the flavor and texture.

instructions.title: Prepare the Pan and Dimple
instructions.text: Generously grease a 9x13 inch baking pan with 3 tablespoons of olive oil. Gently transfer the risen dough into the prepared pan. Using your fingertips, dimple the entire surface of the dough, creating characteristic indentations. Pour the remaining 3 tablespoons of olive oil evenly over the dough.

instructions.title: Second Rise and Topping
instructions.text: Sprinkle the chopped fresh rosemary and coarse sea salt over the dimpled dough. Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap and let it rise again for another 30-45 minutes in a warm spot, allowing the dough to puff up further around the toppings.

instructions.title: Bake the Focaccia
instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 425F or 220C. Bake the focaccia for 25-35 minutes, or until it is golden brown on top and cooked through. The bottom should also be golden and crisp. A good focaccia has a slight chew and a soft interior.

instructions.title: Cool and Serve
instructions.text: Once baked, remove the focaccia from the oven and immediately transfer it to a wire rack to cool slightly. Drizzle with a little extra virgin olive oil if desired. Serve warm or at room temperature, cut into squares or strips. It is delicious on its own or alongside a meal.

tips: For an extra airy focaccia, use a high-hydration dough. The stickier the dough, the lighter the final texture. Do not be tempted to add too much extra flour during mixing.
Do not skimp on the olive oil. The generous amount of good quality extra virgin olive oil is key to the focaccia's rich flavor and crispy crust, especially the oil drizzled before baking.

Focaccia is best enjoyed fresh, ideally on the day it is baked. If you have leftovers, store them at room temperature in an airtight container for up to 2 days, and refresh briefly in a warm oven before serving.
article:
Indulge in a culinary journey to the sun-drenched Ligurian coast of Italy, where the aroma of freshly baked bread, fragrant rosemary, and golden olive oil fills the air. Few baked goods capture the essence of Italian simplicity and flavor as perfectly as Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil. This iconic flatbread, a staple of Genoese cuisine, is more than just bread; it's a testament to the power of a few high-quality ingredients transformed into something extraordinary. With its impossibly light and airy interior, crisp bottom, and a generous slick of robust olive oil infused with aromatic rosemary and coarse sea salt, it's a sensory experience that transports you straight to the heart of Italy.

## What is Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil?

At its core, Focaccia Genovese is a traditional Italian flatbread originating from Genoa, the capital of Liguria. What sets it apart from other focaccias is its unique characteristics: a remarkably thin, almost cracker-like bottom crust, a wonderfully soft and airy crumb riddled with large holes, and the signature dimples that hold pools of flavorful olive oil. The "Genovese" distinction emphasizes this specific style, which is often thinner and crispier than focaccias from other regions. The addition of "Rosemary Olive Oil" highlights the quintessential flavoring elements that make this bread so beloved.

Historically, focaccia served as a breakfast item or a snack for workers, often dipped in coffee or wine. Today, it remains a versatile staple, enjoyed at any time of day. The magic lies in its simplicity. High-quality flour, water, yeast, salt, and, crucially, an abundance of excellent extra virgin olive oil, are the foundation. The olive oil is not merely an ingredient but a star, used generously in the dough, for dimpling, and brushed on top before baking. Fresh rosemary provides a pungent, earthy counterpoint, while coarse sea salt adds a delightful crunch and bursts of salinity, elevating the Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil to a league of its own.

## Ingredients You Need

Crafting authentic Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil doesn't require an extensive shopping list, but the quality of each ingredient makes a significant difference.

*   **High-Quality All-Purpose or Bread Flour:** While some traditional recipes might call for specific Italian '00' flour, a good quality unbleached all-purpose flour or bread flour works beautifully, providing the necessary gluten structure for that airy crumb.
*   **Warm Water:** Essential for activating the yeast. The temperature should be around 105-115°F (40-46°C).
*   **Active Dry or Instant Yeast:** The leavening agent that gives focaccia its lift.
*   **Fine Sea Salt:** Incorporated into the dough for flavor.
*   **Extra Virgin Olive Oil (EVOO):** This is perhaps the most critical ingredient. Invest in a good quality, flavorful EVOO. It's used generously in the dough, for greasing, for dimpling, and for brushing the top. Don't skimp here; the olive oil defines the flavor of your Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** Sprigs of fresh rosemary, finely chopped, are scattered over the top, imparting its distinct, piney aroma and flavor.
*   **Coarse Sea Salt:** A final sprinkle of coarse sea salt (like Maldon or a good flaky sea salt) before baking provides a delightful textural contrast and bursts of briny flavor.

## Step-by-Step Instructions

While exact measurements are best found in a detailed recipe, here’s an overview of the process to create your own Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil:

1.  **Activate the Yeast:** In a large bowl, combine warm water, a pinch of sugar (optional, to feed the yeast), and the yeast. Let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy.
2.  **Form the Dough:** Add the flour, fine sea salt, and a generous glug of extra virgin olive oil to the yeast mixture. Mix until a shaggy dough forms.
3.  **Knead:** Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for about 8-10 minutes until it's smooth, elastic, and passes the windowpane test. Alternatively, use a stand mixer with a dough hook.
4.  **First Rise:** Lightly oil a clean bowl with EVOO, place the dough in it, turning to coat. Cover with plastic wrap and let it rise in a warm place for 1.5-2 hours, or until doubled in size.
5.  **Prepare the Pan:** Liberally oil a baking sheet (a rectangular pan or a cast iron skillet works well) with a generous amount of EVOO. This is crucial for that crispy bottom crust.
6.  **Shape and Dimple:** Gently transfer the risen dough to the prepared pan. Carefully stretch it to fill the pan, trying not to deflate it too much. Using your fingertips, create deep dimples all over the surface of the dough. Pour more EVOO over the top, ensuring it pools in the dimples.
7.  **Second Rise:** Sprinkle the fresh chopped rosemary and coarse sea salt over the dimpled dough. Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap and let it rise again for 30-45 minutes, or until puffy.
8.  **Bake:** Preheat your oven to a high temperature, typically around 400-425°F (200-220°C). Bake for 20-30 minutes, or until the focaccia is golden brown on top and the bottom is deeply golden and crisp.
9.  **Finish:** Once out of the oven, you can brush with a little more EVOO for extra sheen and flavor. Let it cool slightly before slicing and serving.

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

Achieving that perfect Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil requires a few insider tricks:

*   **Hydration is Key:** Focaccia dough is generally high in hydration, meaning it's quite wet and sticky. Don't be tempted to add too much extra flour during kneading, as this will result in a dry, dense bread. Embrace the stickiness; it contributes to the airy texture.
*   **Quality Olive Oil:** We can't stress this enough. The flavor of your focaccia is directly tied to the quality of your extra virgin olive oil. Use a good one, and use it generously. The oil creates a barrier that helps steam the crumb, keeping it moist, and fries the bottom crust to golden perfection.
*   **Deep Dimples:** Don't be shy when dimpling the dough. Use your fingertips to press down firmly, almost to the bottom of the pan. These dimples are essential for trapping the olive oil and creating those beautiful, irregular pockets of air in the crumb.
*   **Hot Oven:** Focaccia loves a hot oven. High heat helps create a quick burst of steam, contributing to the airy interior, and rapidly browns the crust.
*   **Don't Overbake:** Keep an eye on your focaccia. While a golden-brown top is desirable, overbaking can dry it out. The bottom should be deeply golden and crisp, indicating it's cooked through.
*   **Fresh Rosemary & Coarse Salt:** Always use fresh rosemary; dried just won't give the same vibrant flavor. And the coarse sea salt isn't just for looks; it provides a crucial textural and flavor component.

## Serving Suggestions

The beauty of Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil lies in its versatility. While utterly delicious on its own, it’s also a fantastic accompaniment to a variety of dishes:

*   **As a Snack or Appetizer:** Simply tear off a piece and enjoy. It's perfect for dipping in more high-quality olive oil, balsamic vinegar, or a flavorful tapenade.
*   **With Charcuterie and Cheese:** Serve alongside a selection of cured meats like prosciutto or salami, and an assortment of Italian cheeses such as fresh mozzarella, provolone, or parmesan.
*   **Soup and Salad Companion:** Its hearty yet light texture makes it an excellent bread to serve with a warm bowl of minestrone, tomato soup, or a fresh garden salad.
*   **Sandwich Bread:** Slice it horizontally to create unique and flavorful sandwiches. Fill with roasted vegetables, grilled chicken, fresh mozzarella and tomato, or your favorite deli meats.
*   **Breakfast or Brunch:** Traditionally enjoyed in Genoa for breakfast, it pairs surprisingly well with coffee or juice.

## Variations and Substitutions

While the classic Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil is perfect as is, you can certainly experiment with variations to suit your taste:

*   **Other Herbs:** Instead of or in addition to rosemary, try fresh thyme, oregano, or a mix of Italian herbs.
*   **Garlic:** Thinly sliced garlic cloves or roasted garlic mashed into a paste can be scattered over the top for an extra layer of flavor.
*   **Onions:** Caramelized onions or thinly sliced red onions are a popular topping that adds sweetness and depth.
*   **Olives:** Pitted Kalamata or green olives, pressed gently into the dough before the second rise, offer a briny kick.
*   **Cherry Tomatoes:** Halved cherry tomatoes, nestled into the dimples, burst with sweetness when baked.
*   **Cheese:** A sprinkle of grated Parmesan or Pecorino Romano before baking can add a cheesy crust.
*   **Whole Wheat Flour:** For a healthier twist, substitute a portion (up to 50%) of the all-purpose flour with whole wheat flour. This will result in a denser crumb but adds a nutty flavor.
*   **Spices:** A pinch of red pepper flakes can add a subtle heat.

Whether you stick to the traditional Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil or venture into creative variations, the process of making this beloved Italian flatbread is incredibly rewarding. The aroma alone is enough to entice, and the taste is pure, unadulterated pleasure. So roll up your sleeves, embrace the olive oil, and prepare to bake a piece of Italian sunshine right in your own kitchen.
